extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r2 (bradycardia AND pregnancy); rec_drug_b is set true only by r3 (bradycardia). These rules write to different outputs, so the per-output priority/conflict resolution never suppresses either one. With bradycardia=true and pregnancy=true, both r2 and r3 fire: r2 sets rec_drug_a=true and r3 sets rec_drug_b=true. seizure_history is irrelevant (set false here; r1 only affects rec_drug_c). The result has rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b both true, directly violating the mutual exclusion property. The priority scheme only resolves conflicts among rules targeting the same output, so it provides no protection here.
